title: Longest Common Substring of K Strings
description: Find longest substring common across K strings

examples.explanation: K=3 strings. Shortest is 'abcdef' (length 6). Check substrings from longest to shortest: length 6: 'abcdef' not in 'abcfgh' → skip. length 5: 'abcde' not in 'abcfgh'; 'bcdef' not in 'abcfgh'. length 4: 'abcd' not in 'abcfgh'; 'bcde' not in 'abcfgh'; 'cdef' not in 'abcfgh'. length 3: 'abc' found in all strings → return 'abc'.

K=3 strings. Shortest is 'hello' (length 5). Single characters are substrings. 'o' is present in 'hello' (position 4), 'world' (position 1), and 'python' (position 4). No longer common substring exists. Return 'o'.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^K * L)
approaches.summary: Generate all substrings of the shortest string and check each against all other strings using substring search. Use this for small K and short strings due to its simplicity but high cost.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^K * L) where n is string length and L is substring length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Longest Common Substring of K Strings
ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding the longest substring common to all K strings by generating all substrings from the shortest string and checking each against all other strings.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by exhaustively checking all possible substrings. The intuition is that any common substring must be a substring of every string, so we can generate all substrings from one string (choosing the shortest minimizes work) and verify each against the others. This works because we're checking every possibility—if a common substring exists, we'll eventually test it. The algorithm: 1) Identify the shortest string among K strings to minimize substring generation. 2) Generate all possible substrings of the shortest string, from longest to shortest for early exit optimization. 3) For each substring, check if it exists in all other K-1 strings using substring search (like 'in' operator). 4) Return the first (longest) substring found common to all strings, or empty string if none. Time complexity is O(n^K * L) worst-case where n is length of shortest string, K is number of strings, and L is substring length—this comes from generating O(n²) substrings, each checked against K-1 strings with O(L) search per string. Space is O(1) excluding input storage. Connection to L1: This uses the substring verification from L1 as its core checking mechanism. Common mistakes include: not handling empty strings properly, not optimizing by checking longest substrings first, and inefficient substring generation. Real code pattern: def longest_common_substring(strings): shortest = min(strings, key=len); for length in range(len(shortest), 0, -1): for start in range(len(shortest)-length+1): substr = shortest[start:start+length]; if all(substr in s for s in strings if s != shortest): return substr; return ''

examples.trace: K=3 strings. Shortest is 'abcdef' (length 6). Check substrings from longest to shortest: length 6: 'abcdef' not in 'abcfgh' → skip. length 5: 'abcde' not in 'abcfgh'; 'bcdef' not in 'abcfgh'. length 4: 'abcd' not in 'abcfgh'; 'bcde' not in 'abcfgh'; 'cdef' not in 'abcfgh'. length 3: 'abc' found in all strings → return 'abc'.

K=3 strings. Shortest is 'hello' (length 5). Check all substrings from length 5 to 1. No substring of length >1 is common to all three. Single characters like 'o' are common, but the trace checks for length-1 in some cases incorrectly. However, for consistency with problem definition, longest is 1 ('o' or 'l' etc.), but if the problem requires length >1, adjust accordingly. Current output '' is inconsistent with 'o' being common. Fix to return 'o'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Verify Substring in All Strings
ladders.desc: Check if a given substring exists in every one of K strings.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the verification heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: a substring is common to all strings if and only if it can be found within each string. We take a candidate substring and test its presence in each input string. This works because substring existence is a binary property—either the substring appears contiguously in a string or it doesn't. The algorithm: 1) Input: a substring 'candidate' and a list of K strings. 2) For each string in the list, check if 'candidate' is a substring of that string using linear search or built-in methods. 3) If any string doesn't contain the candidate, return False. 4) If all strings contain it, return True. Time complexity is O(K * L * n) worst-case where L is candidate length and n is average string length, but typically O(K * L) with efficient substring search. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in L0 to verify each generated substring. Common mistakes include: using exact equality instead of substring search, not handling empty candidates, and inefficient search algorithms. Real code pattern: def is_common_substring(candidate, strings): return all(candidate in s for s in strings)

examples.trace: Check 'ab' in 'abc': 'ab' found at index 0 → True. Check 'ab' in 'cab': 'ab' found at index 1 → True. Check 'ab' in 'abx': 'ab' found at index 0 → True. All True → return True.
Check 'xyz' in 'abc': not found → False. Since one False, we can short-circuit and return False without checking remaining strings.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Substrings from a String
ladders.desc: Generate every possible contiguous substring of a given string, typically from longest to shortest.
ladders.explanation: This core logic produces all substrings needed for testing. The intuition is that any substring can be defined by its start index and length (or end index). By systematically varying these parameters, we can enumerate all possibilities. This works because there are only O(n²) possible substrings for a string of length n, making exhaustive generation feasible for small n. The algorithm: 1) Input: a string s of length n. 2) For each possible substring length L from n down to 1 (checking longest first for optimization). 3) For each start index i from 0 to n-L. 4) Extract substring s[i:i+L]. 5) Yield or collect the substring. Time complexity is O(n³) if we consider substring extraction cost, but O(n²) substrings generated. Space is O(1) if yielding, O(n²) if storing all. Connection to L1: These generated substrings become candidates verified by L1's routine. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in indices, generating non-contiguous sequences, and inefficient storage. Real code pattern: def generate_substrings(s): n = len(s); for L in range(n, 0, -1): for i in range(n - L + 1): yield s[i:i+L]

examples.trace: n=3. Length 3: start i=0 → 'abc'. Length 2: i=0 → 'ab'; i=1 → 'bc'. Length 1: i=0 → 'a'; i=1 → 'b'; i=2 → 'c'.
n=2. Length 2: i=0 → 'ab'. Length 1: i=0 → 'a'; i=1 → 'b'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Shortest String in a List
ladders.desc: Identify the string with minimum length from a collection of strings.
ladders.explanation: This building block optimizes the brute force approach by reducing the number of substrings generated. The intuition is that the longest common substring cannot be longer than the shortest string, so by starting with the shortest string, we minimize the search space. This works because if we picked a longer string, we'd generate unnecessary longer substrings that couldn't possibly be common. The algorithm: 1) Input: a list of K strings. 2) Initialize 'shortest' as the first string. 3) Iterate through remaining strings, comparing lengths. 4) Update 'shortest' when finding a string with smaller length. 5) Return the shortest string. Time complexity is O(K) to scan all strings. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: The output of this is fed into L2's substring generation. Common mistakes include: not handling empty lists, returning length instead of string, and inefficient comparisons. Real code pattern: def shortest_string(strings): return min(strings, key=len)

examples.trace: Compare lengths: 'apple' length 5, 'banana' length 6, 'cherry' length 6. 'apple' has smallest length 5 → return 'apple'.
Compare lengths: 'longstring' length 10, 'short' length 5, 'medium' length 6. 'short' has smallest length 5 → return 'short'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Substring Existence in a String
ladders.desc: Determine if one string contains another as a contiguous subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation tests whether a pattern string appears within a target string. The intuition is linear scanning: we look for the first character of the pattern in the target, then verify subsequent characters match. This works because strings are sequences where contiguity matters. The algorithm: 1) Input: pattern string 'p' of length m, target string 't' of length n. 2) For each start index i in t from 0 to n-m. 3) Compare p[0] to t[i], if match, compare p[1] to t[i+1], etc., for all m characters. 4) If all match, return True. 5) If no start index yields full match, return False. Time complexity is O(n*m) worst-case but often optimized by built-in methods. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in L3's verification across strings. Common mistakes include: not checking bounds, case sensitivity issues, and using '==' instead of substring search. Real code pattern: def contains_substring(t, p): return p in t

examples.trace: n=11, m=5. Check start i=0: 'h' vs 'w' no match. i=1: 'e' vs 'w' no. i=2: 'l' vs 'w' no. i=3: 'l' vs 'w' no. i=4: 'o' vs 'w' no. i=5: ' ' vs 'w' no. i=6: 'w' vs 'w' match, then 'o' vs 'o' match, 'r' vs 'r' match, 'l' vs 'l' match, 'd' vs 'd' match → all match → return True.
n=3, m=2. Check i=0: 'a' vs 'a' match, but 'b' vs 'c' mismatch. i=1: 'b' vs 'a' mismatch. No valid start → return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Length and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand basic string properties: length and character access by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers fundamental string operations prerequisite for substring work. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters where each position has an index starting at 0. Knowing length tells us how many characters exist, and indexing allows access to individual characters. This works because strings are typically implemented as arrays or similar structures supporting O(1) access. The concepts: 1) Length: number of characters in string, e.g., len('abc') = 3. 2) Indexing: accessing character at position i, e.g., 'abc'[0] = 'a'. 3) Slicing: extracting substring from start to end index, e.g., 'abc'[0:2] = 'ab'. Time complexity for these operations is O(1) for length and indexing, O(k) for slicing where k is slice length. Space is O(1) for access, O(k) for slicing. Connection to L4: These operations enable the substring existence check in L4. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors, negative indices confusion, and misunderstanding slice exclusivity. Real code pattern: s = 'example'; length = len(s); char = s[0]; substring = s[0:3]

examples.trace: String 'hello' has characters: index 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. Length counts all characters: 5. s[0] accesses index 0 → 'h'. s[1:4] slices from index 1 inclusive to 4 exclusive → indices 1,2,3 → 'e','l','l' → 'ell'.
String 'abc': indices 0='a', 1='b', 2='c'. Length=3. s[2] accesses index 2 → 'c'. s[0:2] slices indices 0 to 1 → 'a','b' → 'ab'.

approaches.name: Binary Search with Suffix Array O(K * n log n)
approaches.summary: Build a generalized suffix array for all strings and use binary search on the length of the common substring to find the maximum length. This leverages suffix structures for efficient substring comparison across multiple strings.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(K * n log n) where n is total length of strings
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(K * n) for suffix array and LCP arrays

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Substring of K Strings using Binary Search with Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the longest substring common to all K strings using generalized suffix array and binary search on length.
ladders.explanation: This solution combines multiple advanced data structures to efficiently solve the longest common substring problem for K strings. The core intuition is that if a substring of length L exists in all K strings, then substrings of shorter length also exist, allowing binary search on L. We construct a generalized suffix array containing all suffixes from all strings, then use binary search to find the maximum L where at least one substring of length L appears in every string. For each candidate length L during binary search, we traverse the suffix array and use the longest common prefix (LCP) array to find groups of suffixes that share a common prefix of at least L. Within each group, we check if suffixes come from all K strings. If such a group exists, L is feasible. The algorithm returns the maximum feasible L and can reconstruct the actual substring. Time complexity is O(K * n log n) where n is total string length: O(n log n) for suffix array construction plus O(K * n log n) for binary search with group checking. Space is O(K * n) for suffix array, LCP array, and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of checking feasibility for a given length L. Common mistakes include incorrect string separation in generalized suffix array (need unique sentinels), off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, and inefficient group checking. Real code pattern: 1. Concatenate strings with unique separators. 2. Build suffix array and LCP array. 3. Binary search L from 0 to min(string lengths). 4. For each L, traverse suffix array to find groups with LCP ≥ L. 5. In each group, track which strings are represented. 6. If a group contains all K strings, L is feasible. 7. Return max L and optionally the substring.

examples.trace: 1. Strings: "ababc", "babac", "cabab", total length n=15. 2. Concatenate with separators: "ababc#babac$cabab%" (unique chars #,$,%). 3. Build suffix array SA and LCP array (detailed in L1 example). 4. Binary search on L: min string length = 5, so L range 0-5. 5. L=3 (midpoint): Check feasibility. Traverse SA: Find group with LCP≥3 containing suffixes from all 3 strings. Group starting at index where suffix "bab..." appears: From string1 "ababc" (suffix "babc#..."), string2 "babac" (suffix "babac$..."), string3 "cabab" (suffix "bab%...") all share prefix "bab". All 3 strings present → feasible. 6. L=4: Check, no group with LCP≥4 has all 3 strings → not feasible. 7. Binary search converges to L=3. 8. Return "bab".

1. Strings have no common substring. 2. Concatenate: "abcde#fghij$klmno%". 3. Build SA and LCP. 4. Binary search L: min length=5, range 0-5. 5. L=2: Check groups with LCP≥2. No group contains suffixes from all 3 strings → not feasible. 6. L=1: Check groups with LCP≥1. Still no group with all 3 strings → not feasible. 7. L=0: Always feasible (empty string). 8. Binary search returns L=0, output empty string.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Feasibility of Length L in Generalized Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Determine if there exists a substring of length L common to all K strings using suffix array and LCP array.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine checks whether a given length L is feasible, meaning at least one substring of length L appears in every string. The intuition leverages the suffix array's property: suffixes that share a common prefix appear consecutively in the sorted order. By traversing the suffix array with the LCP array, we can identify groups of suffixes where the longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes is at least L. Within each such group, we check if suffixes originate from all K strings. If any group contains at least one suffix from each string, then L is feasible. Algorithm: 1. Input: concatenated string T, suffix array SA, LCP array, length L, K. 2. Initialize an array or set to track which strings are present in the current group. 3. Traverse SA from i=0 to n-1: a. Determine which original string the suffix SA[i] comes from (using separator positions). b. If LCP[i] (for i>0) < L, start a new group: reset tracking array. c. Add current string to tracking for the group. d. If tracking shows all K strings present, return true. 4. If no group contains all K strings, return false. Time: O(n) per check, where n is total length. Space: O(K) for tracking. Connection to L0: This is the core check within binary search. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle LCP[0] (undefined, treat as 0), incorrect string identification, and not resetting groups properly. Real code pattern: def is_feasible(L, SA, LCP, K, str_id): n = len(SA); present = [False]*K; for i in range(n): if i>0 and LCP[i] < L: present = [False]*K; sid = str_id[SA[i]]; present[sid]=True; if all(present): return True; return False.

examples.input: T="ababc#babac$", SA=[...], LCP=[...], L=2, K=2, strings: "ababc", "babac"
examples.trace: 1. Assume SA sorted suffixes with indices: suffixes from both strings interleaved. 2. L=2. 3. Traverse SA: Start group. i=0: suffix "ababc#..." from string0, LCP[0]=0 (start new group), present[0]=true. i=1: LCP[1]=1 <2, start new group, present reset, suffix "abc#..." from string0, present[0]=true. i=2: LCP[2]=3 ≥2, continue group, suffix "abac$..." from string1, present[1]=true, now present=[true,true] → all strings present, return true.

examples.input: T="abc#def$", SA=[...], LCP=[...], L=3, K=2, strings: "abc", "def"
examples.trace: 1. Strings "abc" and "def" share no common substring of length 3. 2. L=3. 3. Traverse SA: All LCP values <3 (since no common prefixes between strings). 4. Groups never contain both strings. 5. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Length of Common Substring
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find the maximum length L such that a common substring of length L exists across all K strings.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is monotonicity: if a common substring of length L exists, then for any length less than L, a common substring also exists (e.g., take prefix of the L-length substring). Conversely, if no common substring of length L exists, then no common substring of length greater than L exists. This property allows binary search over the possible lengths L from 0 to the minimum string length among the K strings. Algorithm: 1. Let min_len = minimum length of the K strings. 2. Initialize low=0, high=min_len. 3. While low ≤ high: a. mid = (low + high) // 2. b. Check feasibility for length mid using the suffix array method (from L1). c. If feasible, low = mid + 1 (search for longer length). d. Else, high = mid - 1 (search for shorter length). 4. After loop, high is the maximum feasible length. 5. Optionally, find and return the actual substring. Time: O(log(min_len)) * O(feasibility check). Space: O(1) for binary search variables. Connection to L1: Binary search calls the feasibility check subroutine. Common mistakes: Incorrect bounds (should include 0), off-by-one in loop condition, and not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: def binary_search_max_length(strings, K): min_len = min(len(s) for s in strings); low, high = 0, min_len; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if is_feasible(mid): low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return high;

examples.trace: 1. min_len=4 (from "abab"). 2. low=0, high=4. 3. mid=2: Check feasibility L=2 → true (e.g., "ab" common), so low=3. 4. mid=3: Check L=3 → true ("aba" common), so low=4. 5. mid=4: Check L=4 → false, so high=3. 6. low=4, high=3, loop ends. 7. Return high=3.
1. min_len=1. 2. low=0, high=1. 3. mid=0: Check L=0 → always true, so low=1. 4. mid=1: Check L=1 → false, so high=0. 5. low=1, high=0, loop ends. 6. Return high=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Construct Generalized Suffix Array for Multiple Strings
ladders.desc: Build a suffix array for a concatenated string formed from K strings with unique separators.
ladders.explanation: A generalized suffix array extends the suffix array concept to multiple strings by concatenating them with unique separator characters (e.g., '#', '$', '%') that are lexicographically smaller than all regular characters. This ensures suffixes from different strings are compared correctly without mixing across strings. The suffix array is a sorted list of starting indices of all suffixes of the concatenated string. Construction can be done efficiently in O(n log n) time using algorithms like the SA-IS or prefix doubling with radix sort. Intuition: Sorting suffixes allows efficient substring queries because any substring is a prefix of some suffix. For K strings, we concatenate: T = s1 + sep1 + s2 + sep2 + ... + sK + sepK, where sepi are unique and distinct from string characters. Then build SA for T. Additionally, we often need an array to map each suffix to its original string index for later checks. Time: O(n log n) where n is total length including separators. Space: O(n) for SA and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L2: The suffix array enables efficient feasibility checks in binary search. Common mistakes: Using non-unique separators, incorrect lexicographic order, or not handling separators in comparison. Real code pattern: def build_generalized_sa(strings): separators = ['#','$','%','&',...]; T = ''; for i,s in enumerate(strings): T += s + separators[i]; SA = build_suffix_array(T) # using e.g., prefix doubling; return SA, T.

examples.trace: 1. Concatenate: T="ab#ba$" (length 6). 2. Suffixes: indices 0:"ab#ba$",1:"b#ba$",2:"#ba$",3:"ba$",4:"a$",5:"$". 3. Sort suffixes lexicographically: "$" (idx5), "#ba$" (idx2), "a$" (idx4), "ab#ba$" (idx0), "b#ba$" (idx1), "ba$" (idx3). 4. SA = [5,2,4,0,1,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Build Longest Common Prefix (LCP) Array from Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Compute the LCP array where LCP[i] is the length of the longest common prefix between suffixes at SA[i] and SA[i-1].
ladders.explanation: The LCP array is crucial for efficiently finding groups of suffixes that share a common prefix. Given a suffix array SA for a string T of length n, LCP[i] for i from 1 to n-1 is the length of the longest common prefix between the suffixes starting at SA[i] and SA[i-1]. LCP[0] is typically undefined or set to 0. Construction can be done in O(n) time using Kasai's algorithm. Intuition: By leveraging the order of suffixes, we can compute LCP values by comparing characters sequentially while using properties of suffix arrays to avoid redundant comparisons. Algorithm (Kasai's): 1. Compute inverse array rank where rank[SA[i]] = i. 2. Initialize LCP array of size n, set LCP[0]=0. 3. Let k=0. 4. For i from 0 to n-1: a. If rank[i]==0, continue. b. j = SA[rank[i]-1]. c. While i+k < n and j+k < n and T[i+k]==T[j+k], increment k. d. LCP[rank[i]] = k. e. If k>0, decrement k by 1. 5. Return LCP. Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for LCP and rank arrays. Connection to L3: LCP array is built after the suffix array to enable group detection. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in indices, not handling k decrement correctly, or misunderstanding rank array. Real code pattern: def build_lcp(T, SA): n=len(T); rank=[0]*n; for i in range(n): rank[SA[i]]=i; lcp=[0]*n; k=0; for i in range(n): if rank[i]==0: continue; j=SA[rank[i]-1]; while i+k<n and j+k<n and T[i+k]==T[j+k]: k+=1; lcp[rank[i]]=k; if k>0: k-=1; return lcp.

examples.trace: 1. T="ababa" with indices 0-4, SA includes sentinel at end. 2. Compute rank: rank[5]=0, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=2, rank[4]=3, rank[1]=4, rank[3]=5. 3. i=0: rank[0]=1, j=SA[0]=5, compare T[0..]="ababa" and T[5..]="" → k=0, LCP[1]=0. 4. i=1: rank[1]=4, j=SA[3]=4, compare T[1..]="baba" and T[4..]="a" → k=0, LCP[4]=0. 5. i=2: rank[2]=2, j=SA[1]=0, compare T[2..]="aba" and T[0..]="ababa" → common prefix "ab" length 2, but algorithm yields k incrementally: start k=0, match 'a' and 'a' → k=1, match 'b' and 'b' → k=2, mismatch → LCP[2]=2. 6. Continue for all i to get LCP array.

1. T="aaa" indices 0-2, SA includes sentinel at index 3. 2. rank: rank[3]=0, rank[2]=1, rank[1]=2, rank[0]=3. 3. i=0: rank[0]=3, j=SA[2]=1, compare T[0..]="aaa" and T[1..]="aa" → common prefix "aa" length 2, LCP[3]=2. 4. i=1: rank[1]=2, j=SA[1]=2, compare T[1..]="aa" and T[2..]="a" → common prefix "a" length 1, LCP[2]=1. 5. i=2: rank[2]=1, j=SA[0]=3, compare T[2..]="a" and T[3..]="" → k=0, LCP[1]=0. 6. LCP[0]=0 by definition.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Suffix Array and Lexicographic Ordering
ladders.desc: Understand suffix array as a sorted list of all suffixes of a string based on lexicographic order.
ladders.explanation: A suffix array is a fundamental data structure that represents all suffixes of a string in sorted order. For a string T of length n, a suffix is a substring starting at position i and going to the end, denoted T[i..n-1]. The suffix array SA is an array of integers from 0 to n-1 such that the suffixes T[SA[0]..], T[SA[1]..], ..., T[SA[n-1]..] are in lexicographic (dictionary) order. Lexicographic order compares strings character by character: e.g., "apple" < "banana" because 'a' < 'b'; if prefixes match, the shorter string is smaller (or use a sentinel character like '$' that is smaller than all others to handle ties). Intuition: Sorting suffixes allows efficient substring searches because any substring is a prefix of some suffix. Construction can be naive in O(n² log n) by generating all suffixes and sorting, but efficient algorithms achieve O(n log n). Example: For T="banana" with n=6, suffixes: 0:"banana",1:"anana",2:"nana",3:"ana",4:"na",5:"a". Sorted lexicographically: "a" (idx5), "ana" (idx3), "anana" (idx1), "banana" (idx0), "na" (idx4), "nana" (idx2). So SA=[5,3,1,0,4,2]. Time for naive: O(n² log n) due to O(n) comparison per suffix. Space: O(n²) if storing suffixes explicitly, but can be O(n) with indices. Connection to L4: Suffix array is needed to build LCP array. Common mistakes: Confusing suffix array with suffix tree, incorrect lexicographic comparison, or not including all indices. Real code pattern: def naive_suffix_array(T): n=len(T); suffixes = [(T[i:], i) for i in range(n)]; suffixes.sort(key=lambda x: x[0]); SA = [idx for _, idx in suffixes]; return SA.

examples.trace: 1. String T="cab", length n=3. 2. Suffixes: index 0:"cab", 1:"ab", 2:"b". 3. Sort lexicographically: Compare "ab" vs "b": 'a' < 'b' so "ab" < "b". Compare "ab" vs "cab": 'a' < 'c' so "ab" < "cab". Compare "b" vs "cab": 'b' < 'c' so "b" < "cab". 4. Sorted order: "ab" (idx1), "b" (idx2), "cab" (idx0). 5. SA = [1,2,0].

1. T="aab", n=3. 2. Suffixes: 0:"aab", 1:"ab", 2:"b". 3. Sort: Compare "aab" vs "ab": first char 'a' equal, second char 'a' vs 'b': 'a' < 'b' so "aab" < "ab". Compare "ab" vs "b": 'a' < 'b' so "ab" < "b". 4. Sorted: "aab" (idx0), "ab" (idx1), "b" (idx2). 5. SA = [0,1,2].

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(K * n^2)
approaches.summary: Extend the classic LCS DP approach to K strings by maintaining a DP table that tracks common substring lengths ending at positions in each string. This method is intuitive but scales poorly with string length due to quadratic time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(K * n^2) where n is string length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Substring of K Strings using DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of O(K * n²) dynamic programming solution for finding longest substring common to K strings.
ladders.explanation: This solution extends the classic 2-string longest common substring DP approach to K strings. The intuition is that for a substring to be common across all K strings, it must end at some position in each string with matching characters. We maintain a DP table dp[k][i] where k is string index (0 to K-1) and i is position in that string (1-indexed for easier handling). dp[k][i] represents the length of common substring ending at position i in string k that matches the substring ending at position i in string 0. We initialize dp[k][0] = 0 for all k. For each position i in string 0 (1 to n), we check if character at position i in string 0 matches character at position j in string k. If match: dp[k][j] = dp[k-1][j-1] + 1, else dp[k][j] = 0. We track maximum length across all positions. The algorithm works because we're essentially finding the longest diagonal of matching characters across all K strings in the DP space. Time: O(K * n²) where n is string length (worst case when all strings length n). Space: O(K * n) for DP tables. Common mistakes: forgetting to reset dp[k][j] to 0 on mismatch, off-by-one errors in indexing, not handling empty strings. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of updating DP tables across strings.

examples.trace: String lengths: n=6. Initialize dp[3][7] tables (0-indexed with extra row/col). Process i=1 (char 'a' in str0): str1[1]='a' match → dp[1][1]=1, str2[1]='a' match → dp[2][1]=1. i=2 (char 'b'): str1[2]='b' match → dp[1][2]=dp[0][1]+1=2, str2[2]='b' match → dp[2][2]=dp[1][1]+1=2. i=3 (char 'c'): str1[3]='c' match → dp[1][3]=3, str2[3]='c' match → dp[2][3]=3. i=4 (char 'd'): str1[4]='f' mismatch → dp[1][4]=0, str2[4]='i' mismatch → dp[2][4]=0. Max length=3 at i=3, substring 'abc'.
n=5 ('hello'). dp tables updated. At i=4 ('l' in str0), str1[4]='l' match, str2[3]='l' match → dp[2][4]=1. Max length=1 ('l' common to all). Return 'l'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Updating DP Tables Across K Strings
ladders.desc: Core function that updates DP values when comparing string k with reference string.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the K-string extension. Given a reference string (typically string 0) and another string k, along with their current DP tables, it updates dp[k][j] based on character matches. The intuition: dp[k][j] should represent the length of common substring ending at position j in string k that matches the substring ending at position i in the reference string. We iterate through all positions i in reference string and j in string k. If characters match: dp[k][j] = dp[k-1][j-1] + 1 (carrying forward the chain from previous string). If mismatch: dp[k][j] = 0 (breaking the common substring). We need to track maximum across all j. Time: O(n²) for comparing two strings of length n. Space: O(n) for current DP row. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called K-1 times (for strings 1 through K-1) in the full solution. Common mistakes: Using wrong indices for dp[k-1], not handling base cases when j=0 or i=0. Real pattern: for i in range(1, n+1): for j in range(1, n+1): if ref[i-1] == strk[j-1]: dp[k][j] = dp[k-1][j-1] + 1 else: dp[k][j] = 0.

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp_k[4]=[0,0,0,0]. i=1 (ref[0]='a'): j=1 (strk[0]='a') match → dp_k[1]=prev_dp[0]+1=1. j=2 ('x') mismatch → dp_k[2]=0. j=3 ('c') mismatch → 0. i=2 ('b'): j=1 mismatch → 0, j=2 mismatch → 0, j=3 mismatch → 0. i=3 ('c'): j=1 mismatch → 0, j=2 mismatch → 0, j=3 match → dp_k[3]=prev_dp[2]+1=1. Max in dp_k = 1.
n=2. dp_k init zeros. i=1 ('a'): j=1 match → dp_k[1]=1, j=2 mismatch → 0. i=2 ('b'): j=1 mismatch → 0, j=2 match → dp_k[2]=prev_dp[1]+1=2. Max=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Extending 2-String LCS to K Strings
ladders.desc: The fundamental insight of chaining DP tables across multiple strings.
ladders.explanation: The core technique is realizing that for K strings, we can build upon the 2-string solution by maintaining a chain of DP tables. For 2 strings A and B, we compute dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if A[i]=B[j] else 0. For K strings, we treat string 0 as reference and compare it with string 1 to get dp1, then use dp1 as input when comparing with string 2 to get dp2, and so on. dpk[j] represents: 'length of substring ending at j in string k that matches ending at i in string 0, AND also matches in all strings 1..k-1'. This chaining works because dp[k-1][j-1] carries forward the common substring length verified up to string k-1. When we get a match at string k, we increment this chain. Time for K strings: O(K * n²). Space: O(K * n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the update subroutine. Common mistakes: Trying to use 3D DP (O(n^K)), not understanding the chaining dependency. Real pattern: dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(K)] then for k in 1..K-1: update dp[k] using dp[k-1].

examples.trace: n=2. dp0=[0,0,0]. Compare str0&str1: i=1('a'): j=1 match → dp1[1]=1, j=2 mismatch → 0. i=2('b'): j=1 mismatch → 0, j=2 mismatch → 0. dp1=[0,1,0]. Compare str0&str2 using dp1: i=1('a'): j=1 match → dp2[1]=dp1[0]+1=1, j=2 mismatch → 0. i=2('b'): j=1 mismatch → 0, j=2 mismatch → 0. dp2=[0,1,0]. Max across dp2 = 1.
n=3. dp0 zeros. str0&str1: all matches → dp1=[0,1,2,3]. str0&str2 using dp1: matches → dp2=[0,1,2,3]. Max=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Longest Common Substring for 2 Strings using DP
ladders.desc: Classic DP solution for finding longest common substring between two strings.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundation that we extend to K strings. Given strings A and B of length n, we create DP table dp[n+1][n+1] where dp[i][j] = length of common substring ending at A[i-1] and B[j-1]. Initialize first row/col to 0. For i=1..n, j=1..n: if A[i-1]==B[j-1]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1 else dp[i][j]=0. Track maximum dp[i][j]. The intuition: when characters match, we extend the common substring from the previous diagonal position; when mismatch, we reset to 0 because the substring breaks. Time: O(n²). Space: O(n²) but can be optimized to O(n). Connection to L2: This is the k=2 case of the general solution. Common mistakes: Confusing with longest common subsequence, off-by-one in indices. Real pattern: dp=[[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; max_len=0; for i in 1..n: for j in 1..n: if A[i-1]==B[j-1]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1; max_len=max(max_len,dp[i][j]).

examples.trace: n=4. Build dp 5x5. Key matches: A[0]='a' vs B[1]='a' → dp[1][2]=1. A[1]='b' vs B[2]='b' → dp[2][3]=dp[1][2]+1=2. A[2]='a' vs B[3]='a' → dp[3][4]=3. Also other matches. Max dp[3][4]=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison with DP Update
ladders.desc: Fundamental step of checking if two characters match and updating DP value accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation in our DP approach. Given two characters c1 and c2, and a previous DP value prev (from diagonal), we compute new DP value = prev + 1 if c1 == c2 else 0. The intuition: if characters match, we extend the common substring by 1; if they don't match, the common substring ends (length 0). prev represents the length of common substring ending at the previous positions in both strings. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed n² times in the 2-string solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to reset to 0 on mismatch, using wrong prev value. Real pattern: if str1[i] == str2[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 else: dp[i][j] = 0.

examples.trace: Characters match, so new length = prev + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3.
Characters don't match, so new length = 0 (substring breaks).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Common Substring?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic definition of a substring common to multiple strings.
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, in string 'hello', substrings include 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello', 'e', 'el', etc. A common substring of multiple strings is a substring that appears in all of them. For K strings, we're looking for the longest such substring. Important: it must be exact match, same characters in same order. Empty string is technically a common substring of any strings. The intuition: we need to find overlapping regions across all strings. Connection to L4: This concept informs why we compare characters and extend matches. Common mistakes: Confusing with subsequence (which doesn't require contiguity), not considering empty string. Real pattern: Check if a candidate substring exists in all strings using string search.

examples.trace: Candidate substrings of 'cat': 'c','ca','cat','a','at','t'. Check each: 'at' appears in 'cat' (positions 1-2), 'bat' (1-2), 'rat' (1-2). Longer than 'a' or 't'. So 'at' is longest common substring.
Common substrings: 'a' appears in all, 'p' appears in all, 'ap' appears in 'apple' (0-1), 'pineapple' (5-6), 'grape' (2-3). 'ple' not in 'grape'. So longest is 'ap'.

approaches.name: Suffix Automaton Union O(K * n)
approaches.summary: Build a suffix automaton for the first string and traverse it while matching characters from other strings to find the longest common substring. This approach uses automata for linear-time processing per string after initial construction.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(K * n) where n is string length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Substring of K Strings using Suffix Automaton Union
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the longest substring common to K strings using suffix automaton union approach.
ladders.explanation: This solution builds a suffix automaton for the first string, then processes each subsequent string by traversing the automaton while tracking the longest match at each state. The automaton is a finite state machine that represents all substrings of the first string in O(n) space. For each additional string, we simulate matching characters from the automaton's start state, maintaining the current length of the match. At each state, we record the minimum match length across all strings processed so far. After processing all strings, we find the state with the maximum minimum match length, which corresponds to the longest common substring. The intuition is that the automaton compactly represents all substrings, and by tracking match lengths during traversal of other strings, we efficiently compute common substrings without explicitly comparing all possible substrings. Time complexity is O(K * n) where n is the length of the first string (automaton construction is O(n), and each subsequent string traversal is O(n)). Space complexity is O(n) for the automaton. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of traversing the automaton with another string to compute match lengths. Common mistakes include not resetting the match length properly when following suffix links, or incorrectly updating the minimum match lengths across strings. Real code pattern: Build automaton for first string, initialize min_match array for each state with the state's longest substring length, then for each other string, traverse automaton to compute current match lengths and update min_match with minima.

examples.trace: 1. Build suffix automaton for "ababc": States 0-8, edges represent transitions. 2. Initialize min_match for each state: state 0:0, state 1:1, ..., state 8:5 (longest substring lengths). 3. Process "babc": Start at state 0, match 'b' -> state 2, length=1. Update min_match[2]=min(5,1)=1. Continue: 'a' from state 2 -> state 3, length=2, min_match[3]=min(4,2)=2. 'b' from state 3 -> state 4, length=3, min_match[4]=min(3,3)=3. 'c' from state 4 -> state 5, length=4, min_match[5]=min(2,4)=2. 4. Process "abcbc": Start at state 0, 'a' -> state 1, length=1, min_match[1]=min(1,1)=1. 'b' from state 1 -> state 2, length=2, min_match[2]=min(1,2)=1. 'c' from state 2 -> state 6, length=3, min_match[6]=min(3,3)=3. 'b' from state 6 -> state 7, length=4, min_match[7]=min(2,4)=2. 'c' from state 7 -> state 8, length=5, min_match[8]=min(5,5)=5. 5. Find max min_match: state 6 has min_match=3, corresponding to substring "abc" (length 3).
1. Build automaton for 'apple'. 2. Process 'pleat' and 'applet'. 3. Match lengths for state representing 'ple' reach 3 in both strings. Max min_match=3 for state representing 'ple'. Return 'ple'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversing Suffix Automaton with Another String to Compute Match Lengths
ladders.desc: Given a suffix automaton and a string, traverse the automaton to compute the longest match at each state for this string.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine simulates matching the input string against the suffix automaton (built from a reference string). Starting from the initial state, for each character in the input string, we attempt to follow the corresponding transition. If the transition exists, we move to the next state and increment the current match length. If not, we follow suffix links to find a state with the transition, adjusting the match length to the length of that state's longest substring. At each state visited, we record the maximum match length achieved at that state during the traversal. The intuition is that by following the automaton, we efficiently find all matching substrings without backtracking, leveraging the suffix links to handle mismatches. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the input string, as each character causes at most one transition and possibly multiple suffix link follows, but amortized O(1) per character. Space complexity is O(1) extra space plus the automaton size. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each of the K-1 strings after the first to update the minimum match lengths. Common mistakes include not resetting the match length when following suffix links, or incorrectly updating the match lengths for states. Real code pattern: Initialize current state = 0, current length = 0. For each character c in string: while current state has no transition on c and current state is not initial, follow suffix link and set current length = length of current state. If transition exists, move to next state and increment current length. Update match length for the new state.

examples.trace: Automaton states: 0 (initial), 1 via 'a', 2 via 'b', 3 via 'a' from 2, 4 via 'b' from 3. Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 0->2 on 'b'; 1->2 on 'b'; 2->3 on 'a'; 3->4 on 'b'. Process "bab": Start state 0, length 0. Char 'b': transition exists to state 2, length=1. Update match[2]=1. Char 'a': from state 2, transition to state 3, length=2. Update match[3]=2. Char 'b': from state 3, transition to state 4, length=3. Update match[4]=3. Final match lengths: state 0:0, 1:0, 2:1, 3:2, 4:3.

Automaton for "abc": states 0-3. Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'b', 2->3 on 'c'. Process "bc": Start state 0, length 0. Char 'b': no transition from state 0 on 'b'? Actually, automaton for "abc" has transition from 0 to? Check: "abc" automaton: state 0, on 'a' to state 1, on 'b' to state 2? Typically, suffix automaton includes all suffixes, so from state 0, there might be transition on 'b' to state 2? Let's assume standard construction: state 0 has transitions on all characters in string? Actually, suffix automaton for "abc" has states: 0 (initial), 1 after 'a', 2 after 'ab', 3 after 'abc'. Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'b', 2->3 on 'c'. Also, suffix links: 1->0, 2->0, 3->0. For string "bc", start state 0, char 'b': no direct transition from 0 on 'b', so follow suffix link? State 0 has no suffix link (or self), so stay at 0, length=0. Then char 'c': no transition on 'c' from 0, so no match. But "bc" is substring of "abc"? "abc" contains "bc" as suffix. The automaton should have a path for "bc": from state 0, need to get to state representing "bc". Actually, in suffix automaton, state 3 represents "abc", state 2 represents "ab", state 1 represents "a". For "bc", it might be represented by a state reachable via suffix links? Let's correct: Standard suffix automaton for "abc" has more states? Typically, for string "abc", automaton has states: 0 (start), 1 (a), 2 (ab), 3 (abc). Transitions: 0->1 on a, 1->2 on b, 2->3 on c. Also, from state 0, on b? Since "b" is a substring, there should be transition 0-> some state for 'b'. Actually, suffix automaton includes all substrings, so from state 0, there is transition on 'b' to a state representing "b". Let's use a more accurate example: Automaton for "ab" has states: 0, 1 (a), 2 (ab). Transitions: 0->1 on a, 0->2 on b, 1->2 on b. For string "b", traversal: start state 0, char 'b': transition to state 2, length=1. So for "abc", assume transitions: 0->1 on a, 0->2 on b, 0->3 on c? Not exactly. To avoid complexity, use example with "abab" as above. Let's redo example with "abab" automaton and string "ab": Automaton for "abab" as before. String "ab": Start state 0, char 'a': transition to state 1, length=1, match[1]=1. Char 'b': from state 1, transition to state 2, length=2, match[2]=2. Output match lengths: state 0:0, 1:1, 2:2, others 0.

testCases.expected: Match lengths: state for "a" has length 1
Match lengths: state for "est" has length 3
Match lengths: state for "lo" has length 2
Match lengths: state for "ana" has length 3
Match lengths: state for "a" has length 1
Match lengths: state for "b" has length 1
Match lengths: state for "bc" has length 2
Match lengths: state for "aa" has length 2
Match lengths: state for "gram" has length 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Building and Using Suffix Automaton for Substring Representation
ladders.desc: Construct a suffix automaton for a string and understand how it represents all substrings.
ladders.explanation: A suffix automaton is a finite automaton that accepts all suffixes of a string, and by extension, all substrings. It is built incrementally by adding characters one by one. Each state represents a set of end positions of substrings, and transitions represent adding a character. The key properties are: each state has a 'link' (suffix link) to a state representing the longest suffix of the state's strings that appears elsewhere, and each state has a 'length' indicating the longest substring in that state. Construction algorithm: Start with one state (initial). For each character c in the string: create a new state 'cur' with length = last state's length + 1. Then, from the last state, follow suffix links until finding a state with transition on c, and copy transitions to set the automaton. The intuition is that the automaton compresses all substrings into a minimal DFA, allowing efficient substring queries. Time complexity for construction is O(n), space O(n). Connection to L1: This automaton is used as the base for traversing with other strings. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of suffix links during construction, or misassigning lengths. Real code pattern: Use arrays for transitions, link, and length. Iterate through string, update states as per algorithm.

examples.output: Automaton with states: 0 (length 0), 1 (length 1, link 0), 2 (length 2, link 0). Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 0->2 on 'b', 1->2 on 'b'.
examples.trace: Start: state 0, last = 0. Add 'a': create state 1, length=1. From last (0), no transition on 'a', so set transition 0->1 on 'a'. Set link of 1 to 0. Update last = 1. Add 'b': create state 2, length=2. From last (1), no transition on 'b', so set 1->2 on 'b'. Follow suffix link of 1 to 0. From 0, no transition on 'b', so set 0->2 on 'b'. Set link of 2 to 0. Update last = 2.

examples.output: Automaton with states: 0 (length 0), 1 (length 1, link 0), 2 (length 2, link 1). Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'a'.
examples.trace: Start: state 0, last = 0. Add 'a': create state 1, length=1. From last (0), no transition on 'a', set 0->1 on 'a'. Link of 1 to 0. Last = 1. Add 'a': create state 2, length=2. From last (1), has transition on 'a'? No, so set 1->2 on 'a'. Follow suffix link of 1 to 0. From 0, has transition on 'a' to state 1. So, set link of 2 to state 1. Last = 2.

testCases.expected: Automaton with states: 0 and 1, transition on 'a'
Automaton with 3 states, transitions as above
Automaton with states for all substrings
Automaton with states linked in chain
Automaton with suffix links for overlaps

ladders.title: Building Block — Suffix Links in Automata for Efficient Substring Search
ladders.desc: Understand and implement suffix links in an automaton to handle suffix relationships.
ladders.explanation: Suffix links are pointers from a state to another state that represents the longest suffix of the current state's strings that appears in a different position in the string. They are crucial for building the suffix automaton efficiently and for traversing it during matching. When adding a new character, suffix links help find existing states to avoid redundancy. In matching, if a transition is missing, following suffix links allows us to find a state where the match can continue from a shorter suffix. The intuition is that suffix links create a tree structure over states, enabling quick jumps to shorter matches without backtracking through the string. Time complexity for using suffix links is amortized O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(n) for storing links. Connection to L2: Suffix links are a key component in automaton construction and traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect link assignments or not following links during traversal. Real code pattern: Maintain an array 'link' where link[v] points to the state for the longest suffix.

examples.output: Suffix link points to state for "ba"
examples.trace: During construction: After adding "ababa", states include one for "aba" (length 3) and one for "ba" (length 2). The longest suffix of "aba" that appears elsewhere is "ba", so link of "aba" state points to "ba" state.

examples.output: Suffix link points to state for "c"
examples.trace: Automaton for "abc": state for "bc" has length 2. Its longest suffix that appears as a substring in a different position is "c" (since "c" is at end), so link points to state for "c".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding a Character to an Automaton State
ladders.desc: Extend an automaton by adding a new character, creating transitions and updating state information.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves taking the current automaton (built for a prefix of the string) and adding the next character to it. We create a new state for the extended string, set its length, and establish transitions from existing states to this new state based on the character. The key steps are: find the last state, create a new state with incremented length, then iterate through states via suffix links to set transitions. The intuition is that by adding one character, we incorporate all new substrings ending with that character into the automaton. Time complexity is O(1) amortized per character addition. Space complexity is O(1) per addition for the new state. Connection to L3: This operation uses suffix links to efficiently update the automaton. Common mistakes include not handling cases where a transition already exists, or incorrectly setting suffix links. Real code pattern: In a loop for each character c: cur = new state with length = last.length + 1; p = last; while p exists and no transition on c from p, set transition p->cur on c, p = link[p]; then set link of cur.

examples.trace: Last state is for "ab" (state 2). Create new state 3 with length 3. Set p = state 2. From state 2, no transition on 'c', so set transition 2->3 on 'c'. p = link[2] = 0. From state 0, no transition on 'c', so set 0->3 on 'c'. p = link[0] (none). Set link of 3 to 0.

examples.output: New state for "aaa", transitions and links updated
examples.trace: Last state is for "aa" (state 2). Create state 3 with length 3. p = state 2. From state 2, no transition on 'a'? Actually, state 2 has transition on 'a' to? In "aa" automaton, state 2 might have transition? Let's assume from earlier: automaton for "aa" has states 0,1,2 with transitions 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'a'. So from state 2, no transition on 'a', set 2->3 on 'a'. p = link[2] = state 1. From state 1, has transition on 'a' to state 2, so stop. Set link of 3 to state 2? Actually, need to follow algorithm: when p has transition on 'c', we handle differently. Standard algorithm: if p has transition on c to q, then if length[p] + 1 == length[q], set link[cur] = q, else clone state. For simplicity, trace basic addition: after setting transitions, set link of cur to the state reached via p's transition if exists, else to initial. Here, p=1 has transition on 'a' to state 2, and length[1]+1=2 == length[2]=2, so set link[3]=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Finite Automata for String Matching
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of finite automata and how they can be used to match patterns in strings.
ladders.explanation: A finite automaton is a computational model consisting of states and transitions between states based on input characters. It starts in an initial state and processes each character of the input string by following transitions. If it ends in an accepting state, the string is accepted. For string matching, we can design an automaton that accepts strings containing a specific substring or set of substrings. The intuition is that automata provide a way to check for patterns without explicitly comparing all positions, by encoding the pattern into states. Time complexity for processing a string is O(n) where n is the string length, as each character causes one transition. Space complexity depends on the number of states. Connection to L4: This foundational concept is extended to build suffix automata for all substrings. Common mistakes include not defining states properly or missing transitions. Real code pattern: Represent automaton as a graph with adjacency list for transitions. Process string by iterating characters and updating current state.

examples.input: Automaton that accepts strings ending with 'ab', string "cab"
examples.trace: States: 0 (start), 1 (after 'a'), 2 (after 'ab', accepting). Transitions: 0->0 on other chars, 0->1 on 'a'; 1->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'b'; 2->0 on other chars, 2->1 on 'a'. Process "cab": start state 0. 'c': transition to 0. 'a': transition to 1. 'b': transition to 2. End at state 2, which is accepting, so accepted.

Build automaton that accepts strings containing "ana": states for prefixes of "ana". Process "banana": start state 0. 'b': stay in state 0. 'a': go to state 1 (prefix 'a'). 'n': go to state 2 (prefix 'an'). 'a': go to state 3 (prefix 'ana', accepting), match found. Continue: 'n': from state 3, transition? Typically, after match, reset or continue based on design. For simplicity, show matching.
